def_en: To succeed in doing or completing something
def_zh: accomplish a goal; accomplish a task; accomplish a mission
quote_en: She worked for three months to accomplish her goal of running a 5K race.
quote_zh: Every small step is a bridge you build to accomplish the other side of your dream.
ex_en: What you accomplish matters less than who you become while trying.
ex_zh: To accomplish meaningful change, a community must first listen to those it seeks to serve.

def_en: A mixture or joining of two or more things
def_zh: a combination of; perfect combination; powerful combination
quote_en: The recipe requires a combination of flour, sugar, eggs, and butter.
quote_zh: Her laughter was a combination of sunlight and mischief.
ex_en: Success is rarely one thing—it is a combination of effort, timing, and the willingness to try again.
ex_zh: A combination of careful planning and flexible thinking often produces the strongest results.

def_en: Extremely important or necessary
def_zh: crucial role; crucial moment; crucial decision
quote_en: Remembering to charge my laptop was crucial because I had an online presentation at noon.
quote_zh: Trust is the anchor that holds a friendship steady during crucial storms.
ex_en: The crucial difference between wishing and achieving is not talent, but consistent action.
ex_zh: In any debate, it is crucial to acknowledge opposing views before presenting your own argument.

def_en: To make an exact copy of something
def_zh: duplicate a file; duplicate a key; duplicate efforts
quote_en: I accidentally duplicated the photo folder, so now I have two identical copies.
quote_zh: A child's imagination cannot be duplicated by any screen.
ex_en: Instead of trying to duplicate someone else's path, look for the road only you can walk.
ex_zh: Companies often duplicate successful features from competitors, but innovation requires original thinking.

def_en: To come out or appear from a hidden place
def_zh: emerge from; emerge as; begin to emerge
quote_en: The shy boy slowly began to emerge as a confident leader during group projects.
quote_zh: Just as a butterfly must emerge from its cocoon, your truest self appears when you face what scares you.
ex_en: Strength doesn't appear when you need it—it emerges from the small choices you made long before.
ex_zh: After weeks of research, new evidence emerged that completely changed the direction of the study.

def_en: Not relying on others; able to do things for yourself
def_zh: independent thinking; financially independent; independent study
quote_en: She became more independent when she started walking to school by herself.
quote_zh: An independent mind is like a compass that points north even when the crowd goes south.
ex_en: Being independent doesn't mean doing everything alone—it means knowing when to trust yourself and when to ask for help.
ex_zh: A truly independent researcher seeks out feedback, because isolation rarely leads to the best ideas.

def_en: To succeed in dealing with or controlling a problem
def_zh: overcome a challenge; overcome fear; overcome obstacles
quote_en: With help from her friends, she overcame her fear of speaking in front of the class.
quote_zh: Courage is not the absence of fear; it is the bridge you build to overcome it.
ex_en: Every time you overcome a challenge, you grow a little stronger—like a tree that stands firm after a storm.
ex_zh: To overcome systemic issues, one must first understand the root causes rather than just treating symptoms.

def_en: Strange or unusual
def_zh: peculiar habit; peculiar sound; peculiar sense of
quote_en: My dog has a peculiar habit of chasing his own tail whenever he gets excited.
quote_zh: There was a peculiar stillness in the house, as if the walls themselves were listening.
ex_en: What makes you peculiar is what makes you special—embrace the things that set you apart.
ex_zh: The author's peculiar style—mixing poetry with historical footnotes—has earned both criticism and praise.

def_en: To confuse or puzzle someone greatly
def_zh: perplex someone; thoroughly perplexed; perplex the mind
quote_en: The tricky riddle completely perplexed me for almost an hour.
quote_zh: The silence that followed his question perplexed the room into a deeper quiet.
ex_en: When something perplexes you, don't give up—asking questions is how curious minds grow.
ex_zh: The unexpected results of the experiment perplexed the research team, prompting a complete review of their methods.

def_en: Small in number or amount; thinly scattered
def_zh: sparse population; sparse details; sparse vegetation
quote_en: The attendance was sparse on the rainy day, with only half the class showing up.
quote_zh: Hope felt sparse that winter, like a few scattered stars in a vast, dark sky.
ex_en: A forest starts with sparse seeds—but with time and care, it becomes a place of abundance.
ex_zh: The report provided sparse evidence to support its claims, making it difficult for reviewers to accept.

def_en: To signal or call someone with a gesture
def_zh: beckon someone; beckon forward; beckon to
quote_en: My friend beckoned me to come sit next to her when she saved me a seat.
quote_zh: The open road beckoned like a promise written in sunlight.
ex_en: Opportunities often beckon quietly—pay attention, and be brave enough to step forward when they call.
ex_zh: The ambassador beckoned the delegation closer, a gesture that signaled both welcome and urgency.

def_en: To give something as an honor or gift
def_zh: bestow an award; bestow upon; bestow honor
quote_en: The principal will bestow the Good Citizen award to the student who helped the elderly neighbor.
quote_zh: A single act of kindness can bestow warmth that lasts long after the moment is gone.
ex_en: The greatest gift you can bestow upon others is your attention—to listen is to show someone they matter.
ex_zh: The university bestowed an honorary degree on the scientist whose work had inspired generations of students.

def_en: A difficult task or problem that tests someone's abilities
def_zh: face a challenge; accept a challenge; challenge ahead
quote_en: Learning to play the recorder was a challenge at first, but she kept practicing every day.
quote_zh: A challenge is not a wall to stop you—it's a mountain that shows you how strong you can become.
ex_en: You don't grow by avoiding challenges; you grow by walking straight into them and learning to adapt.
ex_zh: The proposal outlined the main challenges the city faces and offered a detailed plan to address each one.

def_en: To become smaller or less
def_zh: decrease gradually; decrease significantly; decrease the risk
quote_en: The number of mistakes in her spelling test decreased after she started reviewing words each night.
quote_zh: As the fog lifted, our worry decreased like a tide pulling back from shore.
ex_en: Worries decrease when you share them with someone who cares—a problem shared is a problem halved.
ex_zh: To decrease traffic congestion, the city implemented a combination of bike lanes and expanded public transit.

def_en: Absolutely necessary; extremely important
def_zh: essential for; essential part; essential element
quote_en: Water and healthy food are essential for staying strong and focused during the school day.
quote_zh: Patience is the soil in which all learning grows; without it, even the best seeds struggle to take root.
ex_en: Practice is essential for growth—every master was once a beginner who refused to give up.
ex_zh: In any effective team, clear communication is essential to avoid misunderstandings and build trust.

def_en: Extremely large; huge
def_zh: gigantic size; gigantic task; a gigantic step
quote_en: The gigantic oak tree in our schoolyard provides shade for everyone during summer sports.
quote_zh: The challenge before them was gigantic, a mountain that seemed to touch the clouds.
ex_en: Even the most gigantic achievements start with one small, brave step forward.
ex_zh: The project required a gigantic effort from the entire community, but the result—a new public park—was worth it.

def_en: With close attention; in a focused way
def_zh: listen intently; stare intently; watch intently
quote_en: The whole class listened intently as the author read an excerpt from her new book.
quote_zh: The cat watched the mouse hole intently, as if the future itself hid inside.
ex_en: When you listen intently, you show respect—and you often learn something you would have otherwise missed.
ex_zh: The detective studied the suspect's expression intently, looking for any flicker of guilt.

def_en: Not thinking too highly of oneself; humble or not large in size
def_zh: modest about; modest improvement; modest house
quote_en: Even though she won the spelling bee, she remained modest and congratulated the other competitors.
quote_zh: A modest heart leaves room to grow; a proud one fills the space with noise.
ex_en: You don't need to shout about your strengths—let your actions speak, and the right people will notice.
ex_zh: The company reported modest gains in the third quarter, but analysts remained optimistic about the long‑term strategy.

def_en: To look like or be similar to someone or something
def_zh: resemble closely; bear a resemblance; strongly resemble
quote_en: Many people say I resemble my dad because we both have curly hair and a big smile.
quote_zh: The fog lay over the valley, resembling a sea of white wool.
ex_en: You don't need to resemble anyone else to be wonderful—being yourself is more than enough.
ex_zh: The new architectural design resembles traditional styles but incorporates modern energy‑efficient materials.

def_en: Serious and dignified; not cheerful or playful
def_zh: solemn promise; solemn expression; solemn ceremony
quote_en: The class grew solemn when they learned that their beloved janitor was retiring after 30 years.
quote_zh: The forest stood solemn, its ancient trees silent witnesses to centuries of change.
ex_en: There is strength in solemn moments—they remind us to be grateful for what truly matters.
ex_zh: The judge's solemn tone conveyed the gravity of the verdict before a single word was spoken.

def_en: Correct and exact; with no mistakes
def_zh: accurate information; accurate measurement; accurate prediction
quote_en: My prediction about the soccer game winner was accurate—our team scored in the last minute.
quote_zh: A memory is not a photograph; it can be accurate in feeling even if the details blur.
ex_en: Being accurate matters, but so does trying—mistakes are simply steps toward getting it right.
ex_zh: For the weather forecast to be accurate, meteorologists rely on data from satellites, radar, and ground stations.

def_en: The customs, arts, and way of life of a particular group
def_zh: local culture; cultural tradition; popular culture
quote_en: Our school celebrates different cultures by having families share traditional food and stories.
quote_zh: A culture of kindness spreads like sunlight; you can't see it move, but everything it touches begins to grow.
ex_en: Every culture has something beautiful to teach us—curiosity about others makes the world a kinder place.
ex_zh: Understanding the culture of a workplace is often as important as having the right skills for the job.

def_en: To take someone's attention away from what they are doing
def_zh: distract from; easily distracted; distract attention
quote_en: The noise from the construction outside tried to distract me, but I focused on finishing my math test.
quote_zh: Her thoughts were a flock of birds, and every new worry distracted another from the sky.
ex_en: Don't let small things distract you from your big dreams—keep your eyes on where you want to go.
ex_zh: Social media can distract students during study time, so some choose to use apps that block notifications.

def_en: Happening often or regularly
def_zh: frequent visits; frequent practice; frequent occurrence
quote_en: After frequent practice sessions, I finally learned to ride my bike without training wheels.
quote_zh: Their laughter was a frequent sound in the old kitchen, bouncing off the walls like sunlight.
ex_en: Frequent effort, even when small, adds up to something remarkable over time.
ex_zh: Frequent breaks during long study sessions can actually improve focus and retention.

def_en: To pause before doing something because of uncertainty
def_zh: hesitate to; hesitate before; never hesitate
quote_en: I started to hesitate before raising my hand, but then I remembered that my answer might help the class.
quote_zh: Opportunity is a door that opens only once; if you hesitate too long, the wind might close it.
ex_en: Don't hesitate to share your ideas—your voice has value, and someone might need to hear it.
ex_zh: When asked to lead the project, she did not hesitate; she knew her team needed direction.

def_en: To make someone extremely angry
def_zh: infuriate someone; infuriate beyond measure; absolutely infuriate
quote_en: It would infuriate me when my little brother borrowed my colored pencils without asking.
quote_zh: The slow crawl of traffic infuriated the morning, turning calm commuters into drumming fingers.
ex_en: When something infuriates you, take a deep breath—calm words speak louder than angry shouts.
ex_zh: The politician's dismissive response to the question infuriated voters and sparked widespread criticism.

def_en: The number of people living in a place
def_zh: growing population; local population; population density
quote_en: The population of our town has grown so much that we are getting a new school next year.
quote_zh: A single act of kindness can shift the atmosphere of an entire population, like a breeze that changes the weather.
ex_en: Every person in a population has a unique gift to offer—a community grows stronger when everyone contributes.
ex_zh: Urban planners study population trends to determine where new schools, hospitals, and parks are needed most.

def_en: Having a sensible and practical view of what is possible
def_zh: realistic goal; realistic expectation; realistic about
quote_en: My goal of reading twenty books this year is realistic because I read one book every two weeks.
quote_zh: Hope without a plan is just a wish; realistic hope is the blueprint of change.
ex_en: Dream big, but set realistic steps along the way—small victories build the path to great success.
ex_zh: The budget proposal was realistic, accounting for unexpected costs while still funding the core programs.

def_en: Something that can be used to help achieve a goal
def_zh: valuable resource; natural resource; limited resource
quote_en: The school library is my favorite resource for finding interesting books about space and planets.
quote_zh: Time is the one resource you can never get back; spend it like it matters.
ex_en: Your greatest resource is your own curiosity—it will guide you to learn anything you wish to know.
ex_zh: The community pooled its resources—volunteers, tools, and materials—to build a new playground in just three weekends.

def_en: To grow, develop, or succeed strongly
def_zh: thrive in; thrive on; continue to thrive
quote_en: My tomato plants thrive in the sunny spot by our classroom window.
quote_zh: A friendship thrives not when there are no disagreements, but when both sides care enough to work through them.
ex_en: When you surround yourself with encouraging people, you will thrive in ways you never imagined.
ex_zh: Small businesses often thrive in communities where residents make a conscious effort to shop locally.

def_en: To tell someone something positively to remove doubt or worry
def_zh: assure someone; rest assured; assure that
quote_en: My teacher assured me that it was okay to make mistakes because that's how we learn.
quote_zh: A calm voice can assure a worried heart like a steady hand on the wheel during a storm.
ex_en: When you assure a friend that they are not alone, you give them a gift stronger than any object.
ex_zh: The company assured customers that their data would remain private, but the recent breach contradicted that promise.

def_en: To understand something fully
def_zh: fully comprehend; difficult to comprehend; comprehend the meaning
quote_en: It took me a few tries to comprehend the instructions for the science experiment.
quote_zh: Some truths, like the vastness of the ocean, can only be felt, never fully comprehended by the mind.
ex_en: To comprehend something new, you must first be brave enough to admit you don't yet know it.
ex_zh: Historians still struggle to comprehend the full impact of the invention on global trade patterns.

def_en: To set up or create something that lasts
def_zh: establish a rule; establish a connection; well established
quote_en: Our class decided to establish a recycling program to help keep our school clean.
quote_zh: A habit is a small river you dig; once established, it flows without effort.
ex_en: Good habits you establish today will serve you well for the rest of your life.
ex_zh: The research team aimed to establish a clear link between diet and cognitive performance in young children.

def_en: To describe something as larger or greater than it really is
def_zh: exaggerate the truth; tend to exaggerate; greatly exaggerated
quote_en: My brother tends to exaggerate when he tells stories about his fishing adventures.
quote_zh: Fear is a lens that exaggerates shadows into monsters.
ex_en: You never need to exaggerate your worth—who you truly are is already enough.
ex_zh: The article exaggerated the risks of the new technology, causing unnecessary public alarm.

def_en: Lacking knowledge or awareness about something
def_zh: ignorant of; blissfully ignorant; remain ignorant
quote_en: I was ignorant about how to care for a pet hamster until I read a book about it.
quote_zh: To be ignorant of one's own ignorance is the deepest shadow of the mind.
ex_en: Being ignorant isn't a weakness—it's an invitation to learn something new every day.
ex_zh: The report criticized the administration for remaining ignorant of the concerns raised by local residents.

def_en: Extremely large or great
def_zh: immense potential; immense size; immense pressure
quote_en: The immense whale at the museum exhibit made me feel tiny standing next to it.
quote_zh: The night sky held an immense silence that seemed to stretch beyond the stars themselves.
ex_en: You have immense potential within you—sometimes it just takes time and patience to discover it.
ex_zh: The project required immense coordination between dozens of teams across three countries.

def_en: A difficult or painful experience
def_zh: difficult ordeal; survive an ordeal; ordeal of
quote_en: The spelling test felt like an ordeal, but I was relieved when I saw my good grade.
quote_zh: What doesn't break you becomes part of your story, and an ordeal can become a turning point.
ex_en: Every ordeal you survive makes you stronger and wiser than you were before.
ex_zh: The family described their ordeal during the flood and thanked the neighbors who helped them escape.

def_en: A careful plan or method for achieving something
def_zh: develop a strategy; winning strategy; long-term strategy
quote_en: My strategy for the math competition was to solve the easier problems first to build confidence.
quote_zh: A strategy without a goal is like a ship without a destination—it moves but never arrives.
ex_en: A good strategy can help you overcome obstacles—but flexibility matters too, because plans can change.
ex_zh: The company's marketing strategy focused on building trust with younger audiences through transparency.

def_en: To gather or call forth something, especially courage or strength
def_zh: summon courage; summon help; summon the strength
quote_en: I had to summon all my courage to ask the principal if I could start a book club.
quote_zh: When words fail, silence can summon understanding in ways speech never could.
ex_en: You can always summon strength you didn't know you had—it lives inside you, waiting to be found.
ex_zh: The governor summoned a special session of the legislature to address the crisis.

def_en: To tease or mock someone in a cruel way
def_zh: taunt someone; cruel taunt; taunt mercilessly
quote_en: The older kids would taunt me for wearing glasses until my friend stood up for me.
quote_zh: The unfinished puzzle sat on the table, each missing piece taunting our efforts.
ex_en: A kind heart does not taunt—it lifts others up, because true strength is gentle.
ex_zh: During the debate, the candidate was careful not to taunt his opponent, knowing it would alienate undecided voters.

def_en: Something accomplished through effort and skill
def_zh: great achievement; sense of achievement; proud achievement
quote_en: Winning the school science fair was my biggest achievement this year.
quote_zh: Achievement is a mirror; it reflects the work you put in, not the luck you had.
ex_en: Every achievement, big or small, is proof that effort turns dreams into reality.
ex_zh: The award recognized her achievements in environmental conservation over the past decade.

def_en: To create or write something, such as music or a piece of writing
def_zh: compose music; compose a letter; compose oneself
quote_en: For our music class, we had to compose a short melody using only three notes.
quote_zh: Her apology was a river she had to compose word by word, flowing from the source of her regret.
ex_en: You compose your own life story every day—make each chapter one you'll be proud to read.
ex_zh: Before sending the email, she took a moment to compose her thoughts, ensuring her tone was professional yet warm.

def_en: Very unusual or remarkable
def_zh: extraordinary talent; extraordinary circumstances; truly extraordinary
quote_en: The firefighter who rescued the kitten from the tall tree did an extraordinary thing.
quote_zh: An ordinary day, looked at with fresh eyes, can become extraordinary.
ex_en: You don't need to be perfect to be extraordinary—you just need to try your best with a kind heart.
ex_zh: The team's extraordinary effort under difficult conditions earned them respect from competitors around the world.

def_en: To stare with an open mouth in surprise or wonder
def_zh: gape at; gape in amazement; leave someone gaping
quote_en: We could only gape in amazement when the magician made a coin disappear right before our eyes.
quote_zh: The canyon below gaped open like a wound in the earth, ancient and patient.
ex_en: Sometimes life will leave you to gape in wonder—those moments of awe remind you how beautiful the world is.
ex_zh: The audience gaped as the dancer seemed to float across the stage, defying gravity with every leap.

def_en: Full of joy and delight
def_zh: gleeful laugh; gleeful expression; gleeful mood
quote_en: The children were gleeful when they saw the first snowfall of the winter.
quote_zh: Her gleeful spirit was contagious, spreading through the room like sunlight breaking through clouds.
ex_en: A gleeful heart makes even ordinary days feel like celebrations—share your joy and watch it multiply.
ex_zh: The volunteers returned from the shelter with gleeful stories of puppies finding their forever homes.

def_en: To recognize or name someone or something
def_zh: identify with; identify the problem; easily identify
quote_en: I can identify different bird species by listening to their songs in the park.
quote_zh: She didn't just see the hero in the story; she identified with the struggle that came before the victory.
ex_en: When you can identify what makes you happy, you've found a compass for your life.
ex_zh: Researchers were able to identify three key factors that contributed to the success of the program.

def_en: To convince someone to do or believe something
def_zh: persuade someone; try to persuade; successfully persuade
quote_en: I tried to persuade my parents to get a puppy by showing them how responsible I could be.
quote_zh: A single story can persuade where a dozen facts fail, because hearts listen when minds resist.
ex_en: The best way to persuade others is not with loud words, but with kind actions that show you care.
ex_zh: The lawyer persuaded the jury by presenting clear evidence and a compelling narrative of events.

def_en: To say what will happen in the future
def_zh: predict the future; accurately predict; predict an outcome
quote_en: It's hard to predict whether it will rain tomorrow, so I always keep an umbrella in my bag.
quote_zh: You can't predict the weather, but you can learn to dance in the rain.
ex_en: You cannot predict every twist in your journey—but you can predict that trying your best will always be worthwhile.
ex_zh: Economists predict that the new policy will create thousands of jobs over the next five years.

def_en: To allow something to happen without complaining; to put up with
def_zh: tolerate behavior; tolerate differences; tolerate pain
quote_en: I can tolerate spicy food much better now than I could at the beginning of the year.
quote_zh: A good friendship tolerates the occasional storm because it trusts the sun will return.
ex_en: Kind people learn to tolerate differences—they know that the world is more beautiful when everyone belongs.
ex_zh: The school's code of conduct states that it will not tolerate bullying in any form.

def_en: Not clear or exact
def_zh: vague memory; vague answer; vague sense of
quote_en: The instructions for the art project were vague, so I asked the teacher to explain again.
quote_zh: The future was a vague shape on the horizon, like mountains seen through morning mist.
ex_en: When your path feels vague, take one small step—clarity often comes as you move forward.
ex_zh: The contract was criticized for being vague about deadlines, leaving room for misinterpretation.

def_en: To say something suddenly without thinking
def_zh: blurt out; blurt an answer; blurt a secret
quote_en: I didn't mean to blurt out the answer, but I was so excited that I knew it.
quote_zh: The truth sometimes blurts itself out before we're ready to speak it.
ex_en: A thoughtless word blurted in anger can take years to mend.
ex_zh: In a formal debate, blurting an unpolished response undermines the speaker's credibility.

def_en: To point out faults or express disapproval
def_zh: criticize harshly; openly criticize; criticize someone
quote_en: It's easier to criticize someone's work than to appreciate the effort they put into it.
quote_zh: The mirror of self‑reflection often criticizes more brutally than any onlooker.
ex_en: Instead of criticizing, try encouraging—a few kind words can help someone grow more than a hundred complaints.
ex_zh: A good reviewer knows how to criticize constructively, offering specific suggestions rather than vague disapproval.

def_en: Weakly made; easily broken or damaged
def_zh: flimsy excuse; flimsy structure; flimsy evidence
quote_en: My first paper airplane was too flimsy to fly, so I used thicker paper for the next one.
quote_zh: The excuse she gave was flimsy, a tissue‑thin barrier against the truth.
ex_en: A flimsy excuse might get you out of trouble, but honesty builds trust that lasts a lifetime.
ex_zh: The prosecution's case rested on flimsy evidence that crumbled under cross‑examination.

def_en: Lucky; having good fortune
def_zh: fortunate enough; incredibly fortunate; fortunate outcome
quote_en: I felt fortunate to have such helpful classmates when I was sick and missed a week of school.
quote_zh: Fortunate are those who, when a door closes, are patient enough to wait for a window to open.
ex_en: The most fortunate people are not those who have the most, but those who appreciate what they have.
ex_zh: It was fortunate that the storm veered away from the coast, sparing the town from disaster.

def_en: To light up or make something clearer
def_zh: illuminate the room; illuminate the issue; illuminate a path
quote_en: The street lamps illuminate the path so I can walk home safely after dark.
quote_zh: A single spark of curiosity can illuminate a whole lifetime of learning.
ex_en: A good book can illuminate ideas you never thought of before, opening doors in your mind.
ex_zh: The professor used simple diagrams to illuminate complex theories for the introductory class.

def_en: To plan or mean to do something
def_zh: intend to; intend for; originally intended
quote_en: I intend to practice the piano every day so I can play a song for my grandmother's birthday.
quote_zh: Words may stumble, but a heart that intends kindness always finds its way.
ex_en: What you intend matters, but what you do matters more—good intentions grow into great actions.
ex_zh: The policy was intended to reduce waste, but its unintended consequences created new problems.

def_en: To copy or imitate someone or something
def_zh: mimic perfectly; mimic the style; mimic sounds
quote_en: My little sister loves to mimic the way I talk, which can be annoying but also funny.
quote_zh: The young bird learned to mimic the dawn chorus, stitching a new song from borrowed notes.
ex_en: Don't just mimic others—find your own voice, because no one else can be you as well as you can.
ex_zh: Some AI systems can mimic human conversation so convincingly that users can't tell they're talking to a machine.

def_en: To mock or make fun of someone in a cruel way
def_zh: ridicule openly; subject to ridicule; face ridicule
quote_en: No one should ridicule another person for asking questions—that's how we all learn.
quote_zh: Fear often hides behind a mask of ridicule, pointing outward to avoid being seen.
ex_en: Those who ridicule others often hide their own fears—true confidence lifts people up.
ex_zh: The proposal was ridiculed at first, but the same ideas later became standard practice in the industry.

def_en: A distinguishing quality or characteristic
def_zh: personality trait; inherited trait; desirable trait
quote_en: One trait I admire in my best friend is her honesty—she always tells me the truth kindly.
quote_zh: Patience is a quiet trait, rarely noticed until the moment it is needed most.
ex_en: Kindness is a trait that costs nothing to give but becomes priceless to those who receive it.
ex_zh: Researchers studied the trait of resilience in children, tracking how they responded to adversity over time.

def_en: To speak or say something aloud
def_zh: utter a word; utter a sound; utter a complaint
quote_en: I was so nervous that I couldn't utter a single word during my presentation.
quote_zh: The forest seemed to hold its breath, as if waiting for someone to utter a spell.
ex_en: When you utter words of encouragement, you plant seeds of courage that grow in someone's heart.
ex_zh: The witness was too shaken to utter a coherent statement until hours after the incident.

def_en: To change or adapt something slightly
def_zh: adjust to; adjust settings; adjust quickly
quote_en: It took me a few weeks to adjust to wearing glasses, but now I don't even notice them.
quote_zh: The sail adjusted to the wind, learning to lean when it could not stand straight.
ex_en: Life often asks you to adjust your plans—flexibility helps you find new paths when old ones change.
ex_zh: After the budget cut, the department had to adjust its hiring plans without sacrificing essential services.

def_en: To agree with or allow something officially
def_zh: approve a request; officially approve; approve of
quote_en: My parents approved my idea to raise money for the animal shelter by selling homemade cookies.
quote_zh: Approval is a warm fire, but seeking it too eagerly can burn away your own judgment.
ex_en: The only approval you truly need is your own when you know you've done your best with a kind heart.
ex_zh: The committee approved the grant after reviewing the applicant's detailed plan and community support.

def_en: To organize and carry out something
def_zh: conduct research; conduct a survey; conduct an experiment
quote_en: Our science class will conduct an experiment to see which plants grow fastest in different lights.
quote_zh: A good leader conducts the group like an orchestra, letting every instrument find its voice.
ex_en: Conduct yourself with kindness and honesty, and you will earn respect that no one can take away.
ex_zh: The organization conducted interviews with over a thousand participants to gather reliable data.

def_en: A disagreement or argument between people
def_zh: resolve a conflict; inner conflict; conflict of interest
quote_en: My friends had a conflict about which game to play, so we took turns choosing.
quote_zh: A conflict within the heart can be louder than any argument shouted across a room.
ex_en: Conflict can be solved when both sides listen—understanding each other is the first step to peace.
ex_zh: The two countries had a long‑standing conflict over water rights, but recent talks have shown promise.

def_en: Equal in value, amount, or meaning
def_zh: equivalent to; roughly equivalent; equivalent value
quote_en: In our math class, we learned that 100 centimeters is equivalent to one meter.
quote_zh: In the currency of kindness, a small gesture can be equivalent to a fortune.
ex_en: Hard work is equivalent to progress—every bit of effort moves you closer to your goal.
ex_zh: The new regulation had an effect equivalent to a tax increase, though it was not presented that way.

def_en: Probably going to happen or be true
def_zh: likely to; more likely; most likely
quote_en: It's likely to rain this afternoon, so I packed my umbrella and raincoat.
quote_zh: A door left ajar is likely to let in not just opportunity but also doubt.
ex_en: You are more likely to succeed when you believe in yourself—confidence opens doors that doubt keeps closed.
ex_zh: The most likely explanation for the discrepancy was a simple data entry error.

def_en: To fail to care for or pay attention to something
def_zh: neglect duties; neglect to mention; neglect a child
quote_en: I promised myself I wouldn't neglect my homework even when there's a new video game to play.
quote_zh: A garden neglected soon forgets its own shape and surrenders to wildness.
ex_en: Don't neglect the small acts of kindness—they often mean the most to someone having a hard day.
ex_zh: The report criticized the agency for neglecting to update its safety protocols despite repeated warnings.

def_en: Not often; rarely
def_zh: seldom seen; seldom used; seldom speak
quote_en: I seldom eat junk food because my mom packs healthy snacks in my lunchbox.
quote_zh: True silence is seldom empty; it is often filled with things we haven't yet learned to say.
ex_en: Words of gratitude are seldom wasted—they cost nothing but can make someone's entire day brighter.
ex_zh: The old library housed books so rare that they were seldom removed from their glass cases.

def_en: Important or large enough to matter
def_zh: significant change; significant impact; significant difference
quote_en: Getting a library card was a significant moment for me—it meant I could borrow any book I wanted.
quote_zh: A single raindrop is not significant, but a thousand together can carve a canyon.
ex_en: Even one person can make a significant difference in the world—never underestimate what you can do.
ex_zh: The study found a significant correlation between sleep duration and academic performance in adolescents.

def_en: Absolutely necessary or essential
def_zh: vital to; vital role; vital information
quote_en: Water and sleep are vital for growing bodies, which is why I try to drink enough and go to bed early.
quote_zh: Trust is the vital breath of any relationship; without it, the whole structure suffocates.
ex_en: A positive attitude is vital for overcoming challenges—it turns obstacles into opportunities to grow.
ex_zh: Accurate weather data is vital for farmers planning planting and harvest schedules.

def_en: To state that something is true without proof
def_zh: claim responsibility; claim ownership; false claim
quote_en: My little brother likes to claim that he caught the biggest fish, even though he was napping.
quote_zh: Silence does not claim victory, but it often leaves the noisiest arguments to prove themselves.
ex_en: Instead of making claims you can't back up, let your actions speak for themselves—they never lie.
ex_zh: The company claimed its product was environmentally friendly, but independent tests proved otherwise.

def_en: The circumstances or setting surrounding a situation or word
def_zh: in context; out of context; historical context
quote_en: When I didn't understand the word, I looked at the context of the sentence to figure out its meaning.
quote_zh: A smile without context is a coin with no value; it could be kindness or a mask.
ex_en: Before judging someone, understand their context—everyone has a story you know nothing about.
ex_zh: The artist's work is often misunderstood when taken out of its cultural context.

def_en: Scattered pieces of waste or remains
def_zh: debris field; remove debris; storm debris
quote_en: After the windstorm, there was debris from branches and leaves all over the playground.
quote_zh: After the argument, their friendship lay in debris, each shard too sharp to touch.
ex_en: Clear away the debris of yesterday's mistakes—they don't belong in the path of your future.
ex_zh: Rescue workers carefully sifted through the debris looking for survivors.

def_en: Strange and frightening
def_zh: eerie silence; eerie feeling; eerie glow
quote_en: The empty school hallway at night looked eerie with only the emergency lights glowing.
quote_zh: There is an eerie beauty in a city asleep, its lights blinking like quiet thoughts.
ex_en: What seems eerie at first can become familiar once you understand it—knowledge chases away fear.
ex_zh: The abandoned hospital had an eerie stillness that made visitors speak in whispers.

def_en: A visual image or design
def_zh: graphic design; computer graphic; striking graphic
quote_en: My poster included colorful graphics that showed the life cycle of a butterfly.
quote_zh: A well‑chosen graphic can speak louder than a paragraph, turning abstract data into a story.
ex_en: A picture can be a graphic that tells a story words cannot—creativity has many languages.
ex_zh: The presentation used clean, simple graphics to make complex information easy to understand.

def_en: To examine or study something carefully
def_zh: investigate thoroughly; investigate a crime; investigate the cause
quote_en: Our class had to investigate why some plants grew taller than others in the school garden.
quote_zh: Curiosity is a detective; it investigates every shadow until the truth steps into light.
ex_en: When you investigate questions you don't understand, you become a detective in the mystery of learning.
ex_zh: The committee was formed to investigate allegations of misconduct and recommend reforms.

def_en: Not following a regular pattern; uneven
def_zh: irregular shape; irregular schedule; irregular heartbeat
quote_en: The irregular shape of the rock made it perfect for skipping across the water.
quote_zh: Life's path is often irregular—full of twists and turns—but that's what makes the journey interesting.
ex_en: Life's path is often irregular—full of twists and turns—but that's what makes the journey interesting.
ex_zh: The patient was referred to a specialist after an irregular heartbeat was detected during a routine checkup.

def_en: To make a soft, crackling sound like leaves or paper
def_zh: leaves rustle; rustle through; rustle softly
quote_en: I could hear the leaves rustle under my feet as I walked through the park.
quote_zh: Secrets rustle through a quiet room like pages of a book no one has opened.
ex_en: When you rustle up courage to try something new, you discover strengths you never knew you had.
ex_zh: The only sound in the library was the occasional rustle of paper as readers turned pages.

def_en: Thin and weak-looking
def_zh: scrawny cat; scrawny arms; scrawny tree
quote_en: The scrawny kitten we found grew into a healthy, fluffy cat after we fed her properly.
quote_zh: A scrawny sapling, if it survives the winter, can one day shelter generations beneath its branches.
ex_en: A scrawny sapling can grow into a mighty oak—with care and time, even the smallest can become strong.
ex_zh: The rescued puppy was scrawny and timid, but after weeks of care it began to thrive.

def_en: Regular payment for work done
def_zh: minimum wage; fair wage; wage increase
quote_en: My older sister saves part of her wage from her part-time job to buy books for the school library.
quote_zh: The wage of patience is not paid in coins, but in moments that would otherwise have been lost.
ex_en: The wage for hard work is not just money—it's the pride that comes from knowing you earned it.
ex_zh: The debate over a living wage has become a central issue in local elections.

def_en: To influence or cause a change in something
def_zh: affect the outcome; deeply affect; affect health
quote_en: The amount of sleep I get really affects how well I do on my spelling tests.
quote_zh: A single kind word can affect a life like a stone dropped into still water—ripples you never see.
ex_en: Your choices affect more than just you—a single kind act can ripple out and touch many lives.
ex_zh: The new tax law will affect small businesses differently than large corporations, according to analysts.

def_en: The complete loss of hope
def_zh: fall into despair; deep despair; overcome despair
quote_en: Even when I felt despair about learning fractions, my tutor helped me understand step by step.
quote_zh: Despair is a tunnel that only seems endless; keep walking, and the light will find you.
ex_en: Despair is a dark cloud that passes—hold on, because sunshine always follows if you wait.
ex_zh: The novel portrays the despair of a community after the factory closure, yet also their resilience.

def_en: To discover or notice something
def_zh: detect a problem; detect changes; detect early
quote_en: My dog can detect the sound of my dad's car from three blocks away.
quote_zh: A true friend can detect the sadness you hide behind a smile.
ex_en: Train your eyes to detect small beauties—a dewdrop on a leaf can hold the whole morning's wonder.
ex_zh: The equipment is sensitive enough to detect minute changes in air pressure.

def_en: On the outside or coming from outside
def_zh: external factors; external appearance; external influence
quote_en: The external appearance of the old house was worn, but the inside was beautifully renovated.
quote_zh: External noise can drown out the quiet voice of your own knowing.
ex_en: External things like prizes fade, but who you are inside—your kindness and courage—lasts forever.
ex_zh: External factors such as weather and market conditions played a significant role in the project's outcome.

def_en: A small, bright flash of light
def_zh: gleam of hope; sudden gleam; gleam in the eye
quote_en: I saw a gleam of sunlight through the clouds after the storm ended.
quote_zh: A gleam of kindness in a stranger's eye can light a path you didn't know you needed.
ex_en: There is a gleam of possibility in every challenge—look for it, and you'll find a way forward.
ex_zh: The detective caught a gleam of recognition in the witness's face before she quickly looked away.

def_en: On the inside; within something
def_zh: internal conflict; internal organs; internal affairs
quote_en: My internal excitement grew as the day of the field trip got closer and closer.
quote_zh: Internal peace is a fortress; external storms may rage, but they cannot break it.
ex_en: True strength comes from internal peace—when you are calm inside, nothing outside can shake you easily.
ex_zh: The company launched an internal investigation after concerns were raised by employees.

def_en: Serious danger
def_zh: great peril; in peril; peril of
quote_en: The sign warned of peril if we climbed the fence near the construction site.
quote_zh: The ship sailed into the peril of uncharted waters, trusting its compass and its crew.
ex_en: Facing peril with courage doesn't mean you aren't scared—it means you move forward anyway.
ex_zh: The rescue mission faced great peril, but the climbers knew the trapped hikers could not wait.

def_en: To limit or control something
def_zh: restrict access; restrict movement; severely restrict
quote_en: My parents restrict my screen time so I have more hours to play outside and read.
quote_zh: A narrow mind restricts the world to fit its own doorway.
ex_en: Don't restrict your dreams to what seems possible—the impossible just takes a little longer.
ex_zh: The new policy restricts the use of personal devices during work hours to reduce distractions.

def_en: Safe and protected
def_zh: feel secure; secure environment; financially secure
quote_en: I feel secure walking to school because there's always a crossing guard at the busy street.
quote_zh: A child's laughter is the sound of a world made secure by love.
ex_en: The most secure feeling comes not from locks, but from knowing you have people who care about you.
ex_zh: The vault was designed to be the most secure in the city, with multiple layers of protection.

def_en: A brief statement of the main points
def_zh: brief summary; executive summary; summary of findings
quote_en: After finishing each chapter, I wrote a short summary to help me remember what happened.
quote_zh: A summary is a window, not the whole house; it lets you see inside without living there.
ex_en: A summary captures the heart of a story—similarly, your best moments capture the heart of who you are.
ex_zh: The report included an executive summary for busy policymakers who needed the key points quickly.

def_en: To gently persuade someone to do something
def_zh: coax out; coax into; coax gently
quote_en: I tried to coax my shy little cousin to join the game by offering to play her favorite card game.
quote_zh: A patient gardener can coax a reluctant seed to break ground with nothing but time and trust.
ex_en: Patience can coax open the most hesitant heart—a gentle nudge is sometimes all someone needs.
ex_zh: The negotiator coaxed both parties back to the table after months of stalled talks.

def_en: To improve or add value to something
def_zh: enhance performance; enhance appearance; enhance understanding
quote_en: Adding colorful pictures will enhance my book report and make it more interesting.
quote_zh: A good friendship enhances both lives, like two mirrors reflecting light between them.
ex_en: Learning new skills doesn't replace who you are—it enhances you, adding new colors to your palette.
ex_zh: The new software enhances productivity by automating routine tasks, allowing staff to focus on creative work.

def_en: To show or display something publicly
def_zh: exhibit artwork; exhibit behavior; exhibit signs
quote_en: Our art class will exhibit our paintings in the school hallway for everyone to see.
quote_zh: The stars exhibit their light without apology, even when no one is watching.
ex_en: You exhibit your character not through words, but through how you treat others every single day.
ex_zh: The museum will exhibit rare artifacts from ancient Egypt for the first time in a decade.

def_en: Well-known from previous experience
def_zh: familiar with; familiar face; familiar surroundings
quote_en: The smell of freshly baked bread was familiar—it reminded me of visiting my grandma.
quote_zh: The familiar ache of missing someone is a map of the love you've carried.
ex_en: Step outside what is familiar once in a while—that's where the greatest discoveries wait.
ex_zh: Though the technology was new, the interface felt familiar to users of the previous version.

def_en: Fierce, violent, or aggressive
def_zh: ferocious attack; ferocious determination; ferocious storm
quote_en: The puppy looked ferocious when he growled at the vacuum cleaner, but he was really just scared.
quote_zh: Her ferocious kindness—fierce and unyielding—refused to let anyone give up on themselves.
ex_en: Be ferocious in pursuing your dreams—not by hurting others, but by never giving up on yourself.
ex_zh: The competition was ferocious, with every team fighting for the championship title.

def_en: Dark and difficult to see through
def_zh: murky water; murky past; murky details
quote_en: The water in the pond was too murky to see the fish swimming below.
quote_zh: The future is a murky lake; you can't see the bottom, but you can still choose to dive in.
ex_en: When the future seems murky, take one step forward—clarity often comes with movement.
ex_zh: The details of the agreement remained murky, leading to speculation and uncertainty.

def_en: The result or consequence of something
def_zh: outcome of; successful outcome; final outcome
quote_en: The outcome of our science fair project was better than I expected—we won second place.
quote_zh: An outcome is a door, not a wall; behind it lies the beginning of something new.
ex_en: You can't always control the outcome, but you can always control the effort you give.
ex_zh: The outcome of the trial will likely influence similar cases for years to come.

def_en: To hold back or control
def_zh: restrain oneself; restrain anger; restrain impulses
quote_en: I had to restrain myself from eating all the cookies so there would be enough for my friends.
quote_zh: Sometimes the strongest hand is the one that learns to restrain, not to strike.
ex_en: Learning to restrain impulsive words takes practice—but it's a skill that builds strong friendships.
ex_zh: The guard had to restrain the enthusiastic crowd to prevent anyone from rushing the stage.

def_en: Feeling or showing sadness
def_zh: sorrowful expression; sorrowful goodbye; deeply sorrowful
quote_en: I felt sorrowful when my best friend moved away, but we still video chat every week.
quote_zh: Sorrowful songs have a strange gift: they make loneliness feel less alone.
ex_en: Sorrowful moments remind us how much we care—they make us appreciate the people we love even more.
ex_zh: The poem ends on a sorrowful note, yet there is a quiet acceptance in its final lines.

def_en: Understanding and sharing another person's feelings
def_zh: express sympathy; deep sympathy; sympathy for
quote_en: My classmates showed sympathy by sending get-well cards when I was home with the flu.
quote_zh: Sympathy is a bridge; it doesn't erase the distance, but it makes crossing possible.
ex_en: Sympathy is seeing someone's pain; kindness is doing something to help lighten their load.
ex_zh: The mayor offered his sympathy to the families affected by the fire and pledged support for rebuilding.

def_en: Suitable or proper for the situation
def_zh: appropriate behavior; appropriate response; appropriate attire
quote_en: Wearing my school uniform was the appropriate choice for the class photo day.
quote_zh: A laugh is not always the appropriate reply to a whisper; sometimes a whisper in return is kinder.
ex_en: Choosing appropriate words shows respect—speak with kindness, and others will listen more willingly.
ex_zh: The committee sought an appropriate balance between preserving history and making room for new development.

def_en: Almost exact but not perfectly accurate
def_zh: approximate value; approximate time; approximate location
quote_en: The approximate number of books in our classroom library is two hundred.
quote_zh: Memory is an approximate map; it shows the shape of things but loses the street names.
ex_en: An approximate answer with effort is better than no answer at all—trying matters more than perfection.
ex_zh: The engineer gave an approximate cost estimate until more detailed plans could be drawn.

def_en: To bring something to an end; to decide after reasoning
def_zh: conclude a meeting; conclude from evidence; conclude successfully
quote_en: We will conclude our unit on space by visiting the science museum next Friday.
quote_zh: Every argument should conclude not with silence but with understanding—even if no one changes their mind.
ex_en: Every ending helps you conclude one chapter so you can begin a new one with fresh energy.
ex_zh: The researchers concluded that the results were inconclusive, calling for further study.

def_en: To burst out suddenly; to explode
def_zh: erupt into laughter; erupt in anger; erupt suddenly
quote_en: Laughter erupted from our classroom when the teacher told a funny joke about a clumsy penguin.
quote_zh: A single spark of injustice can erupt into a wildfire of change.
ex_en: When emotions erupt, take a breath—calm words can cool what angry shouts might burn.
ex_zh: The protest erupted after the announcement, catching city officials off guard.

def_en: Serious, harsh, or gloomy
def_zh: grim expression; grim reality; grim determination
quote_en: The sky looked grim with dark clouds, so we moved our soccer game indoors.
quote_zh: The old photograph held a grim beauty—the faces of people who had endured much and smiled little.
ex_en: Even in grim moments, look for a tiny spark of hope—it can grow into the light you need.
ex_zh: The economic forecast was grim, but community leaders remained focused on solutions.

def_en: To copy someone's actions or speech
def_zh: imitate perfectly; imitate a sound; imitate a style
quote_en: My little brother tries to imitate my basketball moves, even though he can barely reach the hoop.
quote_zh: A student of life does not simply imitate; they listen, learn, and then create their own music.
ex_en: It's okay to imitate those you admire—but add your own twist, because you have something unique to share.
ex_zh: Early painters often imitated the masters as a way to develop their own techniques.

def_en: To demand or state firmly
def_zh: insist on; insist that; insist upon
quote_en: My mom insists that I finish my homework before I can play video games.
quote_zh: Truth does not insist on being heard; it simply waits, patient as stone.
ex_en: When you insist on doing your best, even on hard days, you build a habit that leads to great things.
ex_zh: The witness insisted on telling the whole story, despite the prosecutor's attempts to interrupt.

def_en: To smile or speak in a mocking or scornful way
def_zh: sneer at; dismissive sneer; sneer with contempt
quote_en: The older boy would sneer at my drawings until I entered the art competition and won.
quote_zh: A sneer is often a mask for a wound too tender to show.
ex_en: A sneer can hurt, but kindness heals—choose to be someone who builds others up instead of tearing them down.
ex_zh: The critic sneered at the young author's first novel, a review that sparked public sympathy.

def_en: A small bunch or cluster of something
def_zh: tuft of grass; tuft of hair; tuft of feathers
quote_en: A tuft of grass was growing through a crack in the sidewalk, showing how nature finds a way.
quote_zh: A tuft of stubborn grass on a cliff reminds us that even the smallest hold can anchor life.
ex_en: Like a tuft of grass pushing through concrete, your determination can help you overcome any obstacle.
ex_zh: The bird had a bright red tuft on its head that made it easy to spot among the branches.

def_en: To undo or untangle; to solve or figure out
def_zh: unravel a mystery; unravel a knot; begin to unravel
quote_en: It took me ten minutes to unravel the tangled knot in my shoelace before gym class.
quote_zh: A story, once told, can unravel the tightest knots we carry inside.
ex_en: When problems seem tangled, take time to unravel them slowly—patience solves what rushing cannot.
ex_zh: The detective spent months unraveling the complex web of lies.

def_en: Ready to attack or confront; forceful
def_zh: aggressive behavior; aggressive marketing; aggressive treatment
quote_en: The aggressive player kept trying to steal the ball, so I learned to protect it better.
quote_zh: An aggressive storm does not ask permission; it announces its presence with wind and rain.
ex_en: You can be aggressive in chasing your dreams without being aggressive toward people—strength can be kind.
ex_zh: The company's aggressive expansion strategy led to rapid growth but also significant debt.

def_en: Worried or nervous about something
def_zh: anxious about; anxious feeling; anxious moment
quote_en: I felt anxious before the math test, but deep breaths helped me calm down and focus.
quote_zh: The city before dawn is anxious, waiting for the day to begin like a held breath.
ex_en: Everyone feels anxious sometimes—it's a sign that you care, and courage is moving forward anyway.
ex_zh: Parents grew anxious as the storm delayed the school bus for over an hour.

def_en: Completely confused or puzzled
def_zh: bewildered expression; bewildered look; bewildered by
quote_en: I was bewildered by the complicated instructions for my new board game.
quote_zh: The forest stood silent, its ancient trees gazing down on bewildered travelers.
ex_en: When you feel bewildered, ask questions—curiosity turns confusion into understanding.
ex_zh: The sudden change in policy left employees bewildered and uncertain about their roles.

def_en: The quality of being kind and giving
def_zh: act of generosity; great generosity; spirit of generosity
quote_en: My friend showed generosity by sharing her lunch with me when I forgot mine.
quote_zh: Generosity is a circle; what you give comes back, though often in ways you least expect.
ex_en: Generosity isn't about how much you give—it's about the joy in your heart when you share what you have.
ex_zh: The community's generosity was overwhelming, with donations pouring in from all over the city.

def_en: A deep understanding of something
def_zh: valuable insight; gain insight; provide insight
quote_en: Reading the biography gave me insight into how the inventor came up with her ideas.
quote_zh: Insight is a flashlight in a dark room; it shows you the corners you didn't know were there.
ex_en: Every book offers insight into lives different from your own—reading builds understanding and kindness.
ex_zh: Her insight into the problem helped the team avoid a costly mistake.

def_en: Deserving pity; sadly inadequate
def_zh: pitiful sight; pitiful attempt; pitiful condition
quote_en: The pitiful cries of the lost puppy made everyone in the neighborhood search for its owner.
quote_zh: A pitiful excuse is a small umbrella in a storm—it offers little shelter.
ex_en: A pitiful moment doesn't define your whole story—tomorrow is a new page waiting to be written.
ex_zh: The team's pitiful performance in the first half improved dramatically after the coach's pep talk.

def_en: Most important; main; first in order
def_zh: primary goal; primary reason; primary concern
quote_en: My primary goal this year is to improve my reading skills so I can enjoy longer books.
quote_zh: The primary ingredient of a good friendship is not shared history but shared care.
ex_en: Kindness should be your primary guide—it leads you to choices you'll always be proud of.
ex_zh: The primary objective of the project was to reduce waste, with cost savings a secondary benefit.

def_en: Genuine and honest; without pretense
def_zh: sincere apology; sincere thanks; sincere friendship
quote_en: A sincere apology from my friend made me feel better after our argument.
quote_zh: A sincere heart leaves no fingerprints—it moves without needing to be seen.
ex_en: A sincere compliment costs nothing but can brighten someone's whole day—share them freely.
ex_zh: The letter was short but sincere, expressing gratitude that clearly came from the heart.

def_en: Slow-moving; lacking energy
def_zh: sluggish economy; sluggish response; feel sluggish
quote_en: I felt sluggish in the morning until I ate breakfast and woke up completely.
quote_zh: The river moved sluggish in the heat, as if the sun had pressed pause on its journey.
ex_en: When you feel sluggish, remember that even the slowest turtle finishes the race—keep going.
ex_zh: The company's sluggish reaction to the data breach cost them customer trust.

def_en: To have a strong desire for something
def_zh: yearn for; yearn to; deeply yearn
quote_en: I yearn for the day when I can finally reach the top shelf without asking for help.
quote_zh: A seed yearns for soil, not for a guarantee of sun.
ex_en: Yearn to learn, yearn to grow—a curious heart will take you places you never imagined.
ex_zh: Many young people yearn for meaningful work that makes a difference in their communities.

def_en: To shrink back in fear or embarrassment
def_zh: cringe at; make someone cringe; cringe inwardly
quote_en: I cringed when I remembered the time I called my teacher "Mom" in front of the whole class.
quote_zh: The silence after a bad joke made me cringe more than any laughter could.
ex_en: We all cringe at past mistakes—they're just proof that you're learning and growing every day.
ex_zh: Some viewers cringed at the film's graphic scenes, while others praised its honesty.

def_en: Sadness or disappointment caused by something unexpected
def_zh: to one's dismay; express dismay; fill with dismay
quote_en: To my dismay, the amusement park was closed just as we arrived at the gate.
quote_zh: Dismay is a shadow; it can darken a room, but it cannot lock the door.
ex_en: Dismay is temporary—turn it into determination, and you'll find another path to what you want.
ex_zh: Residents expressed dismay at the sudden closure of the community center they had used for decades.

def_en: Facts or signs that prove something
def_zh: gather evidence; strong evidence; evidence suggests
quote_en: The muddy footprints were evidence that someone had walked through the kitchen.
quote_zh: Evidence is the skeleton of a story; it shows the shape but leaves the feeling to imagination.
ex_en: Kindness leaves evidence too—in the smiles of those you've helped and the warmth you've shared.
ex_zh: The prosecution presented forensic evidence that linked the suspect to the scene.

def_en: To show or communicate a thought or feeling
def_zh: express an opinion; express gratitude; express yourself
quote_en: I used a drawing to express how grateful I was for my teacher's help.
quote_zh: A bird does not need permission to express its song; it sings because the song is in it.
ex_en: Finding healthy ways to express your feelings—through words, art, or music—makes your heart lighter.
ex_zh: The senator expressed strong support for the bill, citing its potential to create jobs.

def_en: To lift or pull up something heavy
def_zh: hoist a flag; hoist a sail; hoist someone up
quote_en: We worked together to hoist the heavy flag to the top of the pole.
quote_zh: Hope is a rope you hoist, one hand over the other, even when you can't see the top.
ex_en: When a task is too heavy to hoist alone, ask for help—teamwork turns heavy loads into shared triumphs.
ex_zh: The crane hoisted the steel beam into place while workers guided it from below.

def_en: To make someone feel ashamed or embarrassed
def_zh: humiliate publicly; feel humiliated; humiliate an opponent
quote_en: The kind boy never tried to humiliate others when they made mistakes in class.
quote_zh: To humiliate another is to admit that your own worth needs the contrast of someone else's shame.
ex_en: No one deserves to be humiliated—choose to be the person who protects others' dignity, not the one who harms it.
ex_zh: The coach was fired after a video surfaced of him trying to humiliate a player during practice.

def_en: Useful and sensible; related to actual practice
def_zh: practical solution; practical experience; practical advice
quote_en: My mom gave me practical advice: pack my backpack the night before to save time in the morning.
quote_zh: Practical wisdom is not found in books alone; it grows in the garden of everyday life.
ex_en: Practical skills like cooking, cleaning, and listening will serve you well for your whole life.
ex_zh: The design won praise for being both beautiful and practical, with storage cleverly hidden.

def_en: Small and weak
def_zh: puny effort; puny size; puny excuse
quote_en: The puny seedling looked fragile, but with water and sunlight it grew taller than me.
quote_zh: A puny spark can ignite a prairie fire if it lands where tinder is dry.
ex_en: Even a puny start can lead to something mighty—don't underestimate what small beginnings can become.
ex_zh: The company's puny marketing budget forced them to rely on word of mouth and creativity.

def_en: To flow or spread slowly through small openings
def_zh: seep through; seep into; seep out
quote_en: Water began to seep through the roof when the rain fell harder than ever.
quote_zh: Doubt can seep into the strongest mind like cold air through a cracked window.
ex_en: Kindness can seep into hearts like sunlight through curtains—a little warmth can change everything.
ex_zh: News of the merger began to seep out ahead of the official announcement.

def_en: Sadly longing for something in the past
def_zh: wistful smile; wistful look; wistful tone
quote_en: I felt wistful looking at photos from kindergarten, remembering how much fun we had.
quote_zh: The old songs played, and a wistful silence settled over the room like dust on forgotten letters.
ex_en: A wistful feeling reminds you of beautiful memories—cherish them, then go make new ones.
ex_zh: She spoke with wistful nostalgia about the neighborhood she had left behind.

def_en: To examine something in detail to understand it
def_zh: analyse data; analyse a problem; critically analyse
quote_en: In science class, we had to analyse the results of our experiment and explain what we learned.
quote_zh: A wise mind analyses its own mistakes the way a mechanic studies an engine—to learn how it works.
ex_en: When you analyse a problem, you turn confusion into a clear path forward—one step at a time.
ex_zh: Economists analysed the data and found a clear correlation between education spending and long‑term growth.

def_en: To suppose something without proof
def_zh: assume responsibility; assume the role; assume the worst
quote_en: I made the mistake of assuming the test would be easy, so I didn't study enough.
quote_zh: To assume you know someone's story is to close the book before reading the ending.
ex_en: Never assume you know someone's story—listen first, because everyone carries things you can't see.
ex_zh: The manager assumed responsibility for the project's delay, even though the issue was outside her control.

def_en: To eat hungrily; to read or absorb quickly
def_zh: devour a meal; devour a book; devour information
quote_en: I was so hungry after swimming that I devoured my sandwich in three bites.
quote_zh: She devoured the novel like a traveler drinking from an oasis after a long desert crossing.
ex_en: When you find a book you love, devour every page—stories have the power to transport you anywhere.
ex_zh: The media devoured the story, running headlines for weeks without waiting for verified facts.

def_en: Feeling jealous of someone else's good fortune
def_zh: envious of; envious glance; make someone envious
quote_en: I felt envious when my friend showed me her new bike, but then she let me ride it.
quote_zh: Envy is a thirst that salt water cannot quench.
ex_en: Instead of feeling envious, celebrate others' successes—their happiness doesn't take anything away from you.
ex_zh: She was not envious of her rival's fame; she simply admired the dedication it took to achieve it.

def_en: To have an effect on someone or something
def_zh: influence decisions; influence behavior; powerful influence
quote_en: My older cousin's love for reading influenced me to try new kinds of books.
quote_zh: A single conversation can influence a life more than a dozen books.
ex_en: Your choices influence more than you know—be the kind of influence that makes the world a little brighter.
ex_zh: The court ruled that the company had tried to influence the jury through improper media statements.

def_en: To forbid something by rule or law
def_zh: prohibit by law; strictly prohibit; prohibit access
quote_en: School rules prohibit running in the hallways to keep everyone safe.
quote_zh: Fear should not prohibit curiosity; a locked door is sometimes an invitation to find a key.
ex_en: Don't let fear prohibit you from trying new things—courage grows when you step outside your comfort zone.
ex_zh: The new ordinance prohibits single‑use plastic bags, encouraging residents to bring reusable ones.

def_en: To ask about someone's private business; to force open
def_zh: pry into; pry open; pry loose
quote_en: I didn't want to pry, so I waited for my friend to tell me what was bothering her.
quote_zh: The truth sometimes resists being pried open, like a shell that holds its pearl.
ex_en: A good friend knows when to listen and when not to pry—respecting boundaries builds trust.
ex_zh: Journalists tried to pry details from the spokesperson, but she refused to comment.

def_en: To remember something from the past
def_zh: recall vividly; recall a memory; fail to recall
quote_en: I tried to recall where I had put my library book, and finally remembered it was under my bed.
quote_zh: Memory does not recall the past; it rebuilds it with whatever materials the present provides.
ex_en: When you recall a kind act someone did for you, let it inspire you to pass that kindness forward.
ex_zh: The witness was unable to recall the license plate number, which complicated the investigation.

def_en: A feeling of sadness about something done or not done
def_zh: deep regret; express regret; regret over
quote_en: My biggest regret was not trying out for the school play—next year I will be brave.
quote_zh: Regret is a door that only opens backward; you can look through, but you cannot step through.
ex_en: Live so that your regrets are few—take chances, speak kindly, and always try your best.
ex_zh: The CEO expressed regret for the layoffs, though she defended the decision as necessary.

def_en: To twist and squeeze; to get something with effort
def_zh: wring out; wring hands; wring from
quote_en: I had to wring out my wet swimsuit before putting it in my bag.
quote_zh: Grief can wring tears from a stone, if you wait long enough.
ex_en: You may wring your hands with worry, but action solves more than worry ever will—take one small step.
ex_zh: The negotiator tried to wring a concession from the other side, but they held firm.

def_en: To tell someone a secret or private matter
def_zh: confide in; confide a secret; confide openly
quote_en: I knew I could confide in my best friend because she always keeps my secrets safe.
quote_zh: A heart that has been heard will confide again; a heart that has been dismissed will close its doors.
ex_en: Being someone others can confide in is a gift—it means you've earned their trust through your kindness.
ex_zh: She confided her fears to her diary, finding comfort in the pages.

def_en: To fear something that is going to happen
def_zh: dread the thought; dread something; live in dread
quote_en: I used to dread giving speeches, but practicing in front of my family made it easier.
quote_zh: What we dread most often grows in the dark; bringing it to light shrinks its power.
ex_en: Don't dread challenges—embrace them as chances to discover how strong you really are.
ex_zh: Many citizens dread tax season, but early preparation can reduce stress.

def_en: A brief or partial view
def_zh: catch a glimpse; fleeting glimpse; glimpse into
quote_en: I caught a glimpse of a deer in the woods before it disappeared behind the trees.
quote_zh: A glimpse of kindness in a stranger's eyes can restore faith you didn't know you'd lost.
ex_en: Every book gives you a glimpse into someone else's world—reading builds bridges between hearts.
ex_zh: The documentary offers a rare glimpse into the daily life of a coral reef scientist.

def_en: To shine with a sparkling light
def_zh: glisten with; glisten in the light; glisten like
quote_en: The morning dew made the spider web glisten like diamonds in the sunlight.
quote_zh: Tears glisten not only with sadness but with the light of the people who care enough to wipe them away.
ex_en: After the rain, everything glistens—sometimes beauty appears after the hardest storms.
ex_zh: The skyscraper's windows glistened as the sun set, reflecting the city's energy.

def_en: To set apart or separate from others
def_zh: isolate from; isolate a problem; socially isolate
quote_en: When I had a cold, I had to isolate myself so I wouldn't spread germs to my classmates.
quote_zh: A single voice, isolated in silence, can still be heard if it sings with enough truth.
ex_en: When you feel isolated, reach out—connection is just a kind word or a listening ear away.
ex_zh: The study aimed to isolate the specific gene responsible for the plant's drought resistance.

def_en: A choice or alternative
def_zh: have an option; consider options; best option
quote_en: For our project, we had the option to make a poster, build a model, or give a presentation.
quote_zh: An option is not a burden; it is a door you can choose to open or leave closed.
ex_en: When life gives you options, choose the one that makes you proud—not just the easiest path.
ex_zh: The committee explored several options before selecting the design that best met the budget.

def_en: A short section from a book; a way through
def_zh: read a passage; narrow passage; passage of time
quote_en: The passage from our reading book described a magical forest full of talking animals.
quote_zh: The passage of time is a river; we cannot step in the same current twice.
ex_en: Every passage you read adds a new room to the house of your imagination—fill it with stories.
ex_zh: The author read a passage from her latest novel to an audience of aspiring writers.

def_en: To smile in an annoying or smug way
def_zh: smirk at; smirk with satisfaction; smirk smugly
quote_en: The bully would smirk when he thought he had tricked someone, but his jokes weren't funny.
quote_zh: A smirk is a mask worn by uncertainty trying to look like certainty.
ex_en: A smirk may seem clever, but a genuine smile costs nothing and spreads real joy.
ex_zh: The candidate smirked during the debate, which many viewers interpreted as arrogance.

def_en: Extremely large in size or extent
def_zh: vast majority; vast expanse; vast difference
quote_en: The vast library had so many books that I could spend years reading and still not finish.
quote_zh: The ocean is vast, yet a single drop contains its salt.
ex_en: You have a vast ocean of potential inside you—explore it, and you'll discover talents you never knew.
ex_zh: The vast majority of participants reported a positive experience with the new program.

def_en: Related to seeing or sight
def_zh: visual aid; visual impact; visual representation
quote_en: The visual instructions with pictures made it much easier to build the birdhouse.
quote_zh: A visual memory is a photograph your mind takes without asking permission.
ex_en: Create a visual of your dreams—a drawing, a collage, a list—and let it remind you where you're going.
ex_zh: The presentation used strong visual elements to keep the audience engaged throughout.
